home.heroSubtitle: The Most Sacred Shakti Peetha of Assam
home.heroTagline: Jai Maa Kamakhya — The Divine Mother of Nilachal Hill
home.welcomeDesc: Nestled atop the Nilachal Hill in Guwahati, Assam, the Kamakhya Temple is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas and one of India's most revered pilgrimage sites. Dedicated to the Goddess Kamakhya — a form of Shakti — this ancient temple radiates divine energy that has attracted millions of devotees for centuries.
home.h1Desc: The annual festival celebrated during the monsoon, signifying the menstruation of Goddess Kamakhya. Thousands of tantric practitioners and devotees gather during this period.
home.h2Desc: Kamakhya is one of the most important Shakti Peethas, believed to be the place where Sati's womb and genitals fell when Lord Vishnu dismembered her body.
home.h3Desc: The temple is a premier seat of Tantric practices in India. The presiding deity is worshipped without a physical form — only a natural rock cleft symbolising the yoni of Devi.
home.h4Desc: The temple opens at 5:30 AM and closes at 10:00 PM. Special darshan timings may vary during festivals. Photography is not permitted inside the main sanctum.

about.title: About Kamakhya Mandir
about.subtitle: A Sacred Shakti Peetha on the Nilachal Hill
about.historyDesc1: The Kamakhya Temple has a rich and ancient history dating back to the 8th–9th century CE. It is believed that the original temple was built by Koch king Nara Narayana in the 17th century after it was demolished by the Kalapahar, a general of Sultan Sulaiman Karrani of Bengal, in 1553 CE.
about.historyDesc2: According to Hindu mythology, the temple is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas — sacred sites created when the body of Sati (Parvati's previous form) was dismembered by Lord Vishnu's Sudarshana Chakra. Kamakhya marks the spot where her womb and genitals fell.
about.architectureDesc: The temple follows the Nilachal style of Assamese architecture. The main shikhara (spire) is beehive-shaped, known as the Khajuraho style. The temple complex consists of several smaller temples dedicated to the ten Mahavidyas: Kali, Tara, Sodashi, Bhuvaneshwari, Bhairavi, Chhinnamasta, Dhumavati, Bagalamukhi, Matangi, and Kamala.
about.significance1: One of the holiest Shakti Peethas in India
about.significance2: Major center of Tantric and Shakta worship
about.significance3: Venue of the grand Ambubachi Mela festival
about.significance4: Houses natural spring water symbolising the creative power of the Devi
about.significance5: Ten Mahavidya temples within the complex
about.locationDesc: Kamakhya Temple is located on Nilachal Hill, Guwahati, Assam 781010.
about.byAir: By Air: Lokpriya Gopinath Bordoloi International Airport (~20 km)
about.byTrain: By Train: Guwahati Railway Station (~8 km)
about.byRoad: By Road: Guwahati is well connected by NH 27 and other highways
